category_title: Students with a history of school failure and deep mistrust of the adult world
student_description: Adam is a student with a history of repeated school failure and a deep mistrust of the adult world, often arriving at school exhausted from difficult home circumstances. He views himself as only being capable of failing, making formal academic demands and classroom transitions highly traumatic. To engage him, educators must prioritize humane, informal relationship-building, explicitly remove traditional academic triggers, and validate his external life challenges.

criteria.rationale: Adam views himself as only good at failing, so he needs explicit reassurance that his past negative labels will not define his current experience.
criteria.check: The tutor's long-term plan includes explicitly communicating that the student has a 'second chance' and that past failures or negative labels are being left behind.

criteria.rationale: Transitions into formal classroom settings are traumatic for Adam, requiring a buffer to ease his anxiety and build rapport.
criteria.check: The tutor establishes regular, non-academic transitional routines (such as meeting over coffee or informal chats) before moving the student into formal instruction.

criteria.rationale: Adam's history of mistrust means he views unexplained rules as arbitrary or unfair, requiring explicit justification to build trust.
criteria.check: The tutor explicitly commits to explaining the reasoning and arguments behind any rules or expectations set for the student.

criteria.rationale: Because Adam may take months to overcome his disappointment in the adult world, he requires a consistently open and accepting environment.
criteria.check: The tutor plans to maintain an 'open door' policy, high availability, or unstructured discussion time to foster a continuously warm and accepting atmosphere.
